questions.question: How is the built-in `Partial<T>` actually implemented in `lib.es5.d.ts`?
options: `{ [P in keyof T]?: T[P] }`
`{ [P in keyof T]-?: T[P] }`
`{ readonly [P in keyof T]: T[P] }`
A compiler intrinsic with no TypeScript source
questions.explanation: `Partial<T>` is a homomorphic mapped type that iterates `keyof T` and stamps the `?` modifier on each property. `{ [P in keyof T]-?: T[P] }` is `Required` (it removes `?`), and there is no intrinsic behind `Partial` — it is one line of TypeScript.

questions.question: Which mapping modifier does `Required<T>` use to make every property required?
options: `-?` (removes the optional marker)
`+?` (adds the optional marker)
`-readonly` (removes readonly)
`?` (adds the optional marker)
questions.explanation: `Required<T>` is `{ [P in keyof T]-?: T[P] }`; the `-?` modifier strips the optional marker. Adding `?` would make properties optional (that is `Partial`), and `-readonly` strips readonly (that is the user-land `Mutable`).

questions.question: `Partial<T>`, `Required<T>`, and `Readonly<T>` recurse into nested objects, making the entire object tree optional/required/readonly.
questions.explanation: All three are shallow — they touch only the top level. A nested object stays mutable/required, which is why you write a `DeepPartial` when you need recursion.

questions.question: `Omit<T, K>` is defined in terms of other utilities. Which definition is the one used in `lib.es5.d.ts`?
options: `Pick<T, Exclude<keyof T, K>>`
`Pick<T, Extract<keyof T, K>>`
`Exclude<Pick<T, K>, keyof T>`
`Record<Exclude<keyof T, K>, T[K]>`
questions.explanation: `Omit<T, K> = Pick<T, Exclude<keyof T, K>>` — compute the surviving keys with `Exclude` (everything in `keyof T` not in `K`), then `Pick` those. Using `Extract` would keep only `K` rather than drop it, inverting the meaning.

questions.question: Which group of utilities are true compiler intrinsics with no readable TypeScript source you could reproduce yourself?
options: `Uppercase`, `Lowercase`, `Capitalize`, `Uncapitalize`, and `NoInfer`
`Partial`, `Required`, `Readonly`, and `Pick`
`Exclude`, `Extract`, and `NonNullable`
`Parameters`, `ReturnType`, and `Awaited`
questions.explanation: The four string-case types plus `NoInfer<T>` are genuine intrinsics. Everything else listed — reshapers, union filters, and `infer`-based extractors — is ordinary TypeScript you can read and rewrite.

questions.question: Given `interface User { id: number; name: string }`, what does `type Oops = Omit<User, "naem">` evaluate to, and does it error?
options: No error; `Oops` equals the full `User` (`{ id: number; name: string }`)
A compile error: `"naem"` is not assignable to `keyof User`
No error; `Oops` is `{ id: number }` (the closest key is removed)
No error; `Oops` is `{}` because the whole type is dropped
questions.explanation: `Omit`'s `K` is constrained only to `keyof any`, not `keyof T`, so a typo'd key is simply absent from `Exclude<keyof T, K>` and nothing is removed — the result is the full type, with no error. `Pick` would error here because its `K extends keyof T`.

questions.question: A teammate writes `type Live = Omit<"draft" | "published" | "deleted", "deleted">` expecting `"draft" | "published"`. Why is this the wrong tool?
options: `Omit` operates on object keys, not union members; to remove a union member you need `Exclude`
`Omit` only accepts a single string key, so passing a union of keys is the error
It works correctly; `Omit` and `Exclude` are interchangeable on unions
`Omit` distributes over the union, so it would yield `never` instead
questions.explanation: `Exclude`/`Extract` work on union members; `Pick`/`Omit` work on object keys. `Omit` over a string-literal union does not filter members the way intended — the correct tool for removing a union member is `Exclude<U, E>`.

questions.question: Given `function make(x: number) { return x; }`, the annotation `const r: ReturnType<make> = 1` compiles fine.
questions.explanation: `make` is a value, but `ReturnType` operates in type space and needs a type. You must write `ReturnType<typeof make>`, using the `typeof` type query to lift the function into type space; `ReturnType<make>` is a type-space-vs-value-space error.

questions.question: What is `Awaited<Promise<Promise<number>>>`, and why?
options: `number`, because `Awaited` is a recursive conditional type that peels every Promise layer, matching runtime `await`
`Promise<number>`, because a single `infer` match unwraps exactly one layer
`Promise<Promise<number>>`, because `Awaited` only affects non-Promise types
`never`, because nested Promises are not assignable to `Promise<infer U>`
questions.explanation: `Awaited<T>` recurses (`T extends Promise<infer U> ? Awaited<U> : T`), so nested Promises collapse to the innermost value type — exactly what runtime `await` does. A naive one-level `infer` would stop at the outer Promise and yield `Promise<number>`.

questions.question: Modern libs define `NonNullable<T>` as `T & {}`. Why does intersecting with the empty object type `{}` drop `null` and `undefined`?
options: `null` and `undefined` are not assignable to `{}`, so for those union members `T & {}` is `never` and they vanish
`{}` is a special token the compiler recognizes as 'strip nullish'
Intersection always discards the left operand's primitive members
`{}` is equivalent to `object`, which excludes all primitives including strings
questions.explanation: Distributing over the union, each non-nullish member intersected with `{}` is itself, but `null & {}` and `undefined & {}` are `never` (they are not assignable to `{}`), so the nullish members drop out. `{}` is not `object` — it accepts any non-null value, including strings and numbers.

questions.question: Why is `Record<string, number>` a weaker choice than `Record<"a" | "b", number>` when the keys are actually a known, finite set?
options: `Record<string, number>` is effectively an index signature: any string key is allowed and (without `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`) reads of absent keys are still typed `number`
`Record<string, number>` forbids reading any key unless it was explicitly assigned first
`Record<string, number>` makes all values optional, while the finite version makes them required
`Record<string, number>` is deep/recursive while the finite version is shallow
questions.explanation: `Record<string, V>` loses key safety — it behaves like a string index signature, allowing any key and typing absent-key reads as `V` (unless `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` is on). A finite key union requires exactly those keys and catches typos.

questions.question: Select all true statements about the function/class extractor utilities.
options: `Parameters<F>` captures the parameter list as a tuple type
`InstanceType<C>` produces the type a `new C(...)` call returns
`ReturnType<any>` is `any` — `any` flows straight through the extractor
`ReturnType<F>` returns a tuple of the function's parameters
`Parameters` and `ReturnType` require a value, so you pass `Parameters<fn>` directly
questions.explanation: `Parameters` yields a (labeled) tuple of params; `InstanceType` yields what `new` produces; and `any` defeats the extractors quietly (`ReturnType<any>` is `any`). `ReturnType` returns the return type, not a param tuple, and these utilities want a type — you write `Parameters<typeof fn>`, not `Parameters<fn>`.

questions.question: Reading the code, what is the resolved type of `Keys`?
questions.code:
type Obj = { a: 1; b: 2; c: 3 };
type Keys = Exclude<keyof Obj, "b">;
options: `never`
questions.explanation: `keyof Obj` is `"a" | "b" | "c"`. `Exclude<U, "b">` distributes: `"a"` and `"c"` survive, `"b" extends "b"` maps to `never`, and `never` is absorbed by the union — leaving `"a" | "c"`.

questions.question: Reading the code, what happens at the two call sites?
options: `b` errors because `C` is inferred as `"red" | "green"` from `opts` alone and `"blue"` is not assignable; `c` compiles with `C = "red" | "green"`
Both compile; `NoInfer` only affects readability and never changes assignability
`b` compiles with `C = "red" | "green" | "blue"`; `c` compiles with `C = "red" | "green"`
Both error because `NoInfer<C>` removes `fallback` from inference, leaving `C` unconstrained
questions.explanation: `NoInfer<C>` removes `fallback` from the inference candidate set, so `C` is decided by `opts` alone (`"red" | "green"`). `fallback` is then merely checked: `"blue"` is not assignable, so `b` errors, while `"red"` is fine. Without `NoInfer`, `fallback` would widen `C` to include `"blue"`.

questions.question: `Exclude`, `Extract`, and `NonNullable` distribute over a union only because the checked type is a naked type parameter; wrapping the checked type in a 1-tuple would block distribution and change the result.
questions.explanation: Distribution happens when the checked type is a naked type parameter and the argument is a union. Blocking distribution (e.g. wrapping in a 1-tuple) makes the conditional evaluate the union as a whole, generally producing a different — usually useless — answer.

questions.question: Given `interface User { id: number; email?: string }`, what is the type of the `email` property in `Required<User>`?
options: `string` — the `-?` modifier removes both the optional marker and the implicit `| undefined`
`string | undefined` — the marker is removed but the union member stays
`string?` — the property type keeps the optional marker
`unknown` — `Required` widens unspecified property types
questions.explanation: `Required` applies the `-?` mapping modifier, which removes the optional marker; the implicit `| undefined` that the optional contributed is also gone, so `email` becomes exactly `string` and a `Required<User>` value must supply a real email.

questions.question: Reading the code, what is the resolved type of `Numbers`?
options: `{ id: number; score: number }`
`{ id: number; name: string; active: boolean; score: number }`
`{ name: string; active: boolean }`
`{ id: never; name: never; active: never; score: never }`
questions.explanation: The `as` key-remapping clause renames each key to itself when `T[K] extends number` and to `never` otherwise; keys remapped to `never` are dropped entirely. Only `id` and `score` have value type `number`, so they survive.

questions.question: Select all correct statements about writing your own utility types.
options: `Mutable<T>` strips readonly using the `-readonly` mapping modifier
`DeepPartial<T>` is a recursive conditional type that re-applies itself to each property's type
`PickByValue<T, V>` filters keys by value type using an `as` clause that maps non-matching keys to `never`
`Mutable<T>` must be a compiler intrinsic because `-readonly` cannot be written in user-land
`DeepPartial<T>` can be written as a single non-recursive mapped type
questions.explanation: `Mutable` uses the `-readonly` modifier, `DeepPartial` recurses through nested objects, and `PickByValue` uses key remapping to `never`. `-readonly` is ordinary user-land syntax (not an intrinsic), and achieving depth inherently requires recursion, so the last two are false.
